case_name: Dual removal from groundwater for small drinking water system
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for groundwater contaminated primarily with Metolachlor intended for drinking water use in a small low-energy system.

input_data.scenario_background: A small rural drinking water utility relies on a shallow groundwater well influenced by nearby agricultural activity and has detected the herbicide Dual in raw water. The utility needs a practical treatment approach to reduce Dual to drinking-water-compliant and protective levels while keeping energy demand and operational burden low. The system has limited staff, prefers compact proven technologies, and can manage routine media changeout and cartridge replacement but not complex concentrate or hazardous residual handling.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve strong reduction of Metolachlor for safe drinking water production and maintain consistent compliance-oriented performance.

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: remove incidental particles and protect downstream adsorption media
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb Metolachlor and other trace organic compounds from groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain carbon fines and provide final particulate polishing
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety in the finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: Metolachlor is a hydrophobic organic pesticide for which activated carbon is a well-established control approach in drinking water treatment
groundwater turbidity is low, so GAC can operate effectively with limited pretreatment
the system has a strong low-energy requirement and GAC avoids high-pressure operation
small utilities commonly use adsorption-based treatment because it is proven, modular, and easier to operate than advanced oxidation or membranes
moderate DOC indicates some competition for adsorption sites but remains within a range where properly designed GAC is still a practical lead option
potential_risks: natural organic matter can shorten carbon bed life and increase replacement frequency
breakthrough can occur if EBCT and monitoring are not adequate
spent carbon management or reactivation logistics must be planned

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: protect downstream solids separation and reduce incidental particulate loading
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: provide adsorptive removal of Metolachlor through dosed carbon contact
core_unit_functions.bag_or_cartridge_filtration: capture PAC solids and polish the treated water

applicability_rationale: PAC is also a recognized treatment option for taste, odor, and many organic micropollutants including pesticides
it can be implemented in compact form where a permanent GAC vessel is not yet installed
energy demand remains low compared with membrane systems
this option can be attractive for seasonal or variable contaminant events when adsorption demand changes
potential_risks: routine chemical handling is needed and may be less convenient for a very small utility
solid residual generation is higher than fixed-bed GAC
performance depends on contact time, dose control, and reliable capture of PAC fines

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: protect oxidation and downstream biofiltration units from particulate fouling
core_unit_functions.ozonation: transform Metolachlor and other oxidizable trace organics into more biodegradable intermediates
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb residual organics and biologically degrade oxidation byproducts
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: polish effluent and control fines or sloughed biomass

applicability_rationale: combined ozone and BAC is an established advanced treatment concept for certain pesticides and trace organic contaminants
the BAC stage provides both adsorption and biological polishing after oxidation
this train can improve resilience if broader micropollutant control becomes necessary
potential_risks: higher operator skill and monitoring are required than for simple GAC
energy demand is higher because of ozone generation
oxidation byproduct formation and biological stability must be managed carefully

core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_uv_hydrogen_peroxide: generate hydroxyl radicals to oxidize Metolachlor and related organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: capture remaining parent compound and oxidation byproducts

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation is technically capable of degrading many pesticides and herbicides
a downstream GAC barrier can improve overall reliability and byproduct polishing
this can be considered where very high treatment assurance for organics is desired
potential_risks: energy use is not aligned with a low-energy small-system preference
chemical storage and UV system maintenance increase operational burden
partial oxidation may generate transformation products that still require downstream removal

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide physical rejection of dissolved organic contaminants including Metolachlor
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: pressure membranes can reduce many dissolved organic micropollutants
this route may be selected when multiple dissolved contaminants also require simultaneous control
potential_risks: high energy demand conflicts directly with the stated low-energy constraint
concentrate management is difficult for a small system with limited residuals options
membrane scaling, cleaning, and post-treatment add unnecessary complexity for a primarily single-organic-contaminant case

must_reflect: small-system suitability and simple routine operation
avoidance of brine or concentrate management
use of proven drinking water treatment approaches for pesticides

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for herbicide removal
how DOC or other matrix effects can influence adsorption performance and media life
why advanced oxidation or membrane approaches rank lower under the stated constraints

should_mention: spent carbon or solids residual management
byproduct or operational complexity concerns for oxidation-based options
